questions.question: Regarding epinephrine administration in adult cardiac arrest, which ONE statement is INCORRECT?
choices: In adult cardiac arrest, consider administering epinephrine 1 mg every 3 to 5 minutes
In PEA/asystole, administer epinephrine as early as possible while maintaining high-quality CPR
In VF/pVT, prioritize defibrillation and CPR as initial management; do not delay defibrillation for drug administration
In VF/pVT, administer epinephrine 1 mg before the first shock
Do not interrupt chest compressions for prolonged periods to administer medications
questions.explanation: In adult cardiac arrest, epinephrine 1 mg is administered every 3 to 5 minutes. In non-shockable rhythms such as PEA/asystole, early administration is considered. In VF/pVT, defibrillation and high-quality CPR take priority as the initial response; the first defibrillation should not be delayed for epinephrine administration. Drug administration should be performed with minimal interruption of chest compressions.

questions.question: Select ALL correct management strategies for defibrillation-refractory VF/pVT.
choices: Reassess depth, rate, recoil, and interruption time of chest compressions
Verify pad contact, placement, and energy settings for defibrillation
Consider administration of amiodarone or lidocaine
Search for reversible causes including hypoxia, hypovolemia, hyperkalemia, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, and pulmonary embolism
After defibrillation, check pulse and rhythm for 2 minutes to confirm ROSC
questions.explanation: After defibrillation in VF/pVT, immediately resume chest compressions and assess rhythm approximately every 2 minutes. For defibrillation-refractory VF/pVT, evaluate CPR quality, pad position, pad contact, defibrillation energy, antiarrhythmic drugs, and reversible causes. Prolonged pulse checks after defibrillation increase chest compression interruptions and are inappropriate.

questions.question: Regarding combinations of reversible causes and initial management in adult cardiac arrest, which ONE pairing is INCORRECT?
choices: When hyperkalemia is suspected, consider calcium salts, insulin-dextrose, and sodium bicarbonate as appropriate for the clinical situation
When tension pneumothorax is suspected, consider chest decompression without waiting for imaging confirmation
When cardiac tamponade is suspected, consider pericardiocentesis or surgical intervention guided by ultrasound findings and clinical history
When hypovolemia is suspected, perform hemorrhage control and fluid/blood transfusion in parallel
In cardiac arrest from hypothermia, defibrillation is absolutely contraindicated and must not be performed until rewarming is complete
questions.explanation: Reversible causes — the Hs and Ts — include hypoxia, hypovolemia, hypo/hyperkalemia and other metabolic disorders, hypothermia, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, toxins, pulmonary thromboembolism, and coronary thrombosis. In hypothermic cardiac arrest, rewarming is essential, but defibrillation is not absolutely contraindicated. In severe hypothermia, defibrillation and drug response may be diminished; adjust the number of defibrillation attempts and drug administration intervals per protocol while rewarming proceeds.

questions.question: Select ALL correct statements regarding management of adult cardiac arrest after endotracheal intubation.
choices: Use waveform capnography to confirm endotracheal tube position
After intubation, continue chest compressions and ventilate at approximately one breath every 6 seconds, i.e., about 10 breaths per minute
Hyperventilation should be avoided because it raises intrathoracic pressure and can impair venous return and coronary perfusion
A sudden rise in ETCO2 may indicate ROSC
After intubation, pause chest compressions with each ventilation to ensure adequate ventilation
questions.explanation: After advanced airway placement, ventilate without interrupting chest compressions, targeting approximately 10 breaths per minute. Waveform capnography is useful for confirming tube position, assessing CPR quality, and detecting ROSC. Hyperventilation raises intrathoracic pressure and can impair venous return and coronary perfusion. Pausing chest compressions with each ventilation is inappropriate.

questions.question: Regarding interpretation of ETCO2 during adult cardiac arrest, which ONE statement is INCORRECT?
choices: ETCO2 during chest compressions serves as a surrogate marker for pulmonary blood flow and cardiac output to assess CPR quality
A sudden rise in ETCO2 suggests the possibility of ROSC
Persistently low ETCO2 warrants reassessment of CPR quality, ventilation, tube position, and reversible causes
If ETCO2 is below 10 mmHg after 20 minutes of CPR, this value alone is sufficient to decide to terminate resuscitation
Waveform capnography is useful for tube position confirmation because esophageal intubation results in non-sustained waveforms
questions.explanation: ETCO2 is useful for assessing CPR quality, pulmonary blood flow, and detecting ROSC. ETCO2 persistently below 10 mmHg during CPR should prompt reassessment of compression quality and search for reversible causes. However, ETCO2 alone should not be used to decide to terminate resuscitation; the decision requires integration of factors such as witness status, initial rhythm, no-flow/low-flow time, reversible causes, temperature, and response to treatment.

questions.question: Select ALL correct statements about drug administration during cardiac arrest.
choices: When VF/pVT fails to respond to defibrillation, consider amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO with an additional 150 mg as a second dose
When amiodarone is unavailable or an alternative is preferable, lidocaine may be considered
For polymorphic ventricular tachycardia suspected to be torsades de pointes, consider magnesium sulfate
In cardiac arrest, routinely administer calcium salts regardless of cause
Calcium salts may be considered when hyperkalemia, calcium channel blocker toxicity, or hypocalcemia is suspected
questions.explanation: For defibrillation-refractory VF/pVT, consider amiodarone or lidocaine. The representative dose of amiodarone is 300 mg as an initial bolus with 150 mg as an additional dose. Consider magnesium sulfate for torsades de pointes. Calcium salts are not routinely administered to all cardiac arrest patients; they should be considered when there is a specific indication such as hyperkalemia, calcium channel blocker toxicity, or hypocalcemia.

questions.question: Regarding management of PEA/asystole, which ONE statement is INCORRECT?
choices: Continue high-quality CPR and minimize chest compression interruptions
Administer epinephrine 1 mg as early as possible and consider repeating every 3 to 5 minutes
During rhythm checks, verify whether the rhythm has changed to VF/pVT
Because defibrillation can restore spontaneous circulation in PEA, perform defibrillation as the first response
Search for and treat reversible causes including hypoxia, hypovolemia, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, and pulmonary embolism
questions.explanation: PEA/asystole are non-shockable rhythms; initial management consists of high-quality CPR, early epinephrine administration, and identification and treatment of reversible causes. Defibrillation is used for VF/pVT, not PEA/asystole. During rhythm checks, verify whether the rhythm has converted to VF/pVT.

questions.question: Select ALL correct statements about cardiac arrest in pregnancy.
choices: Do not delay high-quality CPR or defibrillation when indicated, as in standard adult ALS
Perform manual left uterine displacement to relieve aortocaval compression by the gravid uterus
For pregnancies at 20 weeks or more, or when the uterine fundus is at or above the umbilicus, consider perimortem cesarean delivery as part of early emergency obstetric management
To minimize risk to the fetus, use lower-than-standard defibrillation energy in maternal cardiac arrest
If ROSC is not achieved approximately 4 minutes after cardiac arrest, coordinate with the obstetric team and aim for delivery by approximately 5 minutes
questions.explanation: High-quality CPR and defibrillation should not be delayed in pregnant patients. Defibrillation energy is managed the same as in standard adults. At ≥20 weeks of gestation, or when the uterine fundus is at or above the umbilicus, aortocaval compression impairs resuscitation; manual left uterine displacement should be performed. If ROSC is not achieved approximately 4 minutes after cardiac arrest, aim for delivery by approximately 5 minutes through perimortem cesarean delivery (resuscitative hysterotomy). This procedure improves maternal circulation as well as providing the opportunity for fetal survival.

questions.question: Regarding post-ROSC management, which ONE statement is INCORRECT?
choices: To avoid both hypoxia and hyperoxia, titrate supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 of approximately 92 to 98%
To avoid hypotension, target a systolic BP of at least 90 mmHg or mean arterial pressure of at least 65 mmHg
If altered mental status persists, manage temperature and prevent fever
If ST-elevation myocardial infarction is suspected, consider coronary reperfusion therapy
Immediately after ROSC, target SpO2 100% for prolonged periods to maximize cerebral oxygenation
questions.explanation: After ROSC, avoid both hypoxia and hyperoxia; titrate oxygen to target SpO2 approximately 92 to 98%. Circulatory management targets SBP ≥90 mmHg or MAP ≥65 mmHg to prevent hypotension. When altered mental status persists, practice targeted temperature management (TTM) and prevent fever. When cardiac etiology is suspected, particularly with ST elevation, consider coronary reperfusion.

questions.question: Regarding the use of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) during cardiac arrest, which ONE statement is INCORRECT?
choices: POCUS may help identify reversible causes including cardiac tamponade, right heart dilation suggesting pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax, and severe hypovolemia
POCUS should be performed within the brief pause for rhythm or pulse checks and must not prolong chest compression interruptions
Even if cardiac motion is completely absent on POCUS in PEA, POCUS findings alone should not immediately justify termination of resuscitation
Even if POCUS does not identify the cause, continue high-quality CPR and standard ALS
If needed to evaluate the image adequately, it is acceptable to interrupt chest compressions for approximately 1 minute
questions.explanation: POCUS can be useful for identifying reversible causes in cardiac arrest, but must not prolong chest compression interruptions. It should be limited to the brief window during rhythm checks; prolonged interruptions for image acquisition are inappropriate. Additionally, POCUS findings alone should not be used to terminate resuscitation; decisions require integration of clinical course, initial rhythm, no-flow/low-flow time, reversible causes, and treatment response.
